data_in.description: Raw LC-MS/MS spectral data files (e.g., mzML, mzXML, or vendor-specific formats)

data_out.description: Serialized specXplore session data object file saved to disk

domain_knowledge: specXplore session data objects are binary or structured containers that encapsulate processed spectral metadata, feature arrays, and similarity embeddings for downstream dashboard visualization.
The two-stage workflow separates data processing (stage 1: Jupyter) from interactive exploration (stage 2: dashboard), enabling reproducible preprocessing and reusable session files.

Raw spectral input formats commonly include vendor-specific binary formats (e.g., .raw, .d) or open standards (mzML, mzXML); the importing pipeline must parse and normalize these into a common internal representation.
Session serialization preserves computed embeddings (e.g., t-SNE coordinates) and similarity scores to avoid recomputation during dashboard interaction.

direct_checks: verify that kevinmildau/specXplore repository is accessible at github.com/kevinmildau/specXplore
verify file_exists: README or documentation file in repository root describing two-stage importing pipeline
verify file_exists: Jupyter notebook example file(s) in repository demonstrating spectral data import
verify script_runs: execute provided example Jupyter notebook without errors on sample LC-MS/MS spectral data (input format and sample data location must be documented in README)
verify file_exists: serialised session data object output file with documented file extension/format (e.g. .pkl, .h5, .json) after notebook execution
verify format_is: output session data object conforms to documented specXplore session object schema
verify contains_substring: output object contains expected fields for spectral similarities, embeddings, or fragmentation data as documented
expert_review: assess whether the two-stage architecture (Jupyter import → session object → dashboard) is correctly implemented and matches the claimed workflow separation
assess whether the serialised session object structure is appropriate for downstream dashboard consumption and contains sufficient metadata for reproducible exploration
assess whether example notebooks adequately document parameter choices, preprocessing steps, and data transformations applied during import

expected_outputs: Serialized specXplore session data object file saved to disk
finding: The specXplore importing pipeline operates in Jupyter notebooks and accepts raw spectral data as input, producing a serialized specXplore session data object that is saved to the hard drive for downstream consumption by the dashboard session instance.
inputs: Raw LC-MS/MS spectral data files (e.g., mzML, mzXML, or vendor-specific formats)
methodology_summary: Initialize Python environment and Jupyter notebook with specXplore importing pipeline module.
Load raw LC-MS/MS spectral data files into memory using specXplore's data import functions.
Parse and normalize spectral records to extract peaks, intensities, and metadata.
Apply importing pipeline processing steps to construct the session data object.
Serialize the session object to disk in a format compatible with specXplore dashboard consumers.
Validation: confirm session file exists, contains expected data structures (spectral arrays, embeddings, metadata), and can be deserialized without error by downstream dashboard application.

workflow_description: 1. Launch Jupyter notebook environment and initialize the specXplore importing pipeline module. 2. Load raw LC-MS/MS spectral data files into the notebook using specXplore's data import functions. 3. Process and validate the spectral data through the importing pipeline to create a specXplore session data object. 4. Serialize the session data object and save it to disk as a persistent file.
